name: Oxford Institute for Ethics in AI — White Paper (April 2025)
description: Responsible use of GenAI in adult social care. 150+ participant summit.
statistic: The Oxford Institute for Ethics in AI published 'The responsible use of Generative AI in adult social care: A value-led approach' in April 2025, following the first-ever AI in Social Care Summit (27 March 2025 at Rhodes House, University of Oxford) convening 150+ participants from adult social care, civil society, people with lived experience, academia, policy, and technology sectors. The white paper defines responsible AI in social care as systems that 'support and do not undermine, harm or unfairly breach fundamental values of care, including human rights, independence, choice and control, dignity, equality and wellbeing.' It produced six key recommendations: (1) adopt ethical guidance on generative AI in care settings, (2) ensure diverse stakeholder involvement, (3) develop regulatory frameworks with government, (4) build infrastructure for human-centred care technology, (5) provide government backing for care technology business models, (6) align National Technology Standards with legal and ethical frameworks.
source: Oxford Institute for Ethics in AI, University of Oxford (co-authored with Digital Care Hub and Casson Consulting)
date: April 2025 (white paper publication); 27 March 2025 (AI in Social Care Summit)
credibility: Academic / sector body — University of Oxford's Institute for Ethics in AI is a globally recognised centre. Co-produced with Digital Care Hub (the sector's digital skills body) and Casson Consulting, with input from NHS England and the Department of Health and Social Care. First summit of its kind in the UK.
applicability: Directly care sector — specifically focused on adult social care and generative AI. Addresses care homes, domiciliary care, and supported living contexts. Produced with and for the social care sector.
citable: Yes — freely available PDF from the University of Oxford. Attribute to 'Oxford Institute for Ethics in AI, The responsible use of Generative AI in adult social care, April 2025.' Strong credibility for a care sector audience given the Oxford brand and cross-sector authorship.
website_placement: Trust and credibility section — the Oxford brand and 150+ participant summit add authority. Works well in a 'Responsible AI' or 'Our Approach' page to demonstrate that Clarica's AI work aligns with the sector's ethical framework. The six recommendations could be presented as a checklist that Clarica's services help providers meet. Also suitable for blog content exploring each recommendation.
emotional_vs_rational: Both — emotional appeal through the values-led framing (human rights, dignity, independence, wellbeing) which resonates with care professionals' intrinsic motivation. Rational appeal through the structured recommendations and regulatory alignment that gives decision-makers a framework for responsible adoption.
clarica_differentiator: Very strong differentiator. This white paper provides the ethical foundation that Clarica can explicitly align with — positioning the agency as not just technically capable but ethically grounded. The emphasis on 'human-centred care technology' and the need for government-backed business models validates Clarica's consultative approach. Clarica can reference this framework to demonstrate that its AI solutions are built in accordance with the sector's own ethical standards, distinguishing it from generic AI vendors who lack social care domain expertise.
refresh_frequency: One-off publication, but the Oxford Institute for Ethics in AI maintains an ongoing research programme. The Digital Care Hub continues to develop practical guidance. Follow-up publications and events are likely as the sector matures. Monitor the Institute's publications page for updates.
comparison_pair: Before: No formal ethical framework existed for generative AI use specifically in adult social care. After: A cross-sector, values-led framework with six actionable recommendations co-produced by 150+ stakeholders including people with lived experience. This sets the benchmark for responsible AI adoption in the sector.
